original_abstract: Efficient C-C bond cleavage and oxidation of alcohols to CO2 is the key to developing highly efficient alcohol fuel cells for renewable energy applications. In this work, we report the synthesis of core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) with stepped Pt clusters deposited along the ultrathin (2.3 nm) stepped Au NWs as an active catalyst to effectively oxidize alcohols to CO2. The catalytic oxidation reaction is dependent on the Au/Pt ratios, and the Au1.0/Pt0.2 NWs have the largest percentage (∼75%) of stepped Au/Pt sites and show the highest activity for ethanol electro-oxidation, reaching an unprecedented 196.9 A/mgPt (32.5 A/mgPt+Au). This NW catalyst is also active in catalyzing the oxidation of other primary alcohols, such as methanol, n-propanol, and ethylene glycol. In situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy and infrared spectroscopy are used to characterize the catalyst structure and to identify key reaction intermediates, providing concrete evidence that the synergy between the low-coordinated Pt sites and the stepped Au NWs is essential to catalyze the alcohol oxidation reaction, which is further supported by DFT calculations that the C-C bond cleavage is indeed enhanced on the undercoordinated Pt-Au surface. Our study provides important evidence that a core/shell structure with stepped core/shell sites is essential to enhance electrochemical oxidation of alcohols and will also be central to understanding electro-oxidation reactions and to the future development of highly efficient direct alcohol fuel cells for renewable energy applications.
humanized_abstract: Efficient C-C bond cleavage and oxidation of alcohols to CO2 is highly desired for direct alcohol fuel cells (DAFCs) based on renewable energy. Here, we developed a new core/shell Au/Pt nanowires (NWs) catalyst with stepped Pt clusters deposited along the ultrathin (2.3 nm) stepped Au NWs for highly efficient alcohol oxidation to CO2. The Au/Pt ratios influence significantly the catalytic oxidation reaction toward CO2. At Au1.0/Pt0.2 catalyst, about 75% stepped Au/Pt sites were reserved and demonstrated the highest ethanol electro-oxidation activity with the current density of 196.9 A/mgPt (32.5 A/mgPt+Au). In addition, other primary alcohols including methanol, n-propanol and ethylene glycol were also well-oxidized using this unique NW catalyst structure. In-situ X-ray absorption spectroscopy (XAS) and in-situ infrared spectroscopy have been carried out to study the catalyst structure and the intermediates in the oxidation reaction. These experiment results along with density functional theory (DFT) calculation demonstrate that the synergistic effect between low-coordinated Pt sites and stepped Au structure of the ultrathin Au NWs plays a crucial role for efficient alcohol oxidation reaction, and C-C bond cleavage was enhanced on the undercoordinated Pt-Au surface. Therefore, our research confirms that the unique core/shell structure with stepped core/shell sites is promising for efficient electrochemical oxidation of alcohols and provides further fundamental knowledge for other related electro-oxidation reactions, and thus has great potentials for highly efficient direct alcohol fuel cells (DAFCs) to realize renewable energy applications.
